coredata.dc:title: Efficient scalable co-precipitated carbon dots and silver bromide doped manganese dioxide for catalytic and antimicrobial activity with evidential in-silico analysis

Ahmed, Bilal Anjum
coredata.dc:description: In this research, a co-precipitation approach was used to synthesize manganese oxide (MnO2) nanorods (NRs) doped with varying concentrations (2, 4 wt.%) of silver bromide (AgBr) and fixed amounts of carbon dots (CDs). CDs exhibit high photostability and a high electron transfer rate. AgBr can improve the activity of metal oxide owing to its electron-trapping effect. The new characteristics of as-prepared AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 NRs have a larger surface area, charge transport capabilities, and lower recombination chances, associating the synthesized material as a suitable choice for environmental cleanup. The removal of organic dye pollutants from wastewater and the elimination of harmful bacteria by the use of antibacterial activity were the goals of this research. The NRs demonstrated outstanding catalytic activity (CA) against rhodamine B (RhB) dye degradation. AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 nanocatalyst indicates efficient dye degradation (96.39 %) in an acidic medium relative to other basic and neutral media. The fundamental components for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) are electrons and hole pairs responsible for killing pathogenic bacteria. Compared to ciprofloxacin the substantial inhibition zones against Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria were measured at 2.95 mm. In silico molecular docking study of AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 NRs proposed as promising DNA gyrase E. coli inhibitors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, a co-precipitation approach was used to synthesize manganese oxide (MnO2) nanorods (NRs) doped with varying concentrations (2, 4 wt.%) of silver bromide (AgBr) and fixed amounts of carbon dots (CDs). CDs exhibit high photostability and a high electron transfer rate. AgBr can improve the activity of metal oxide owing to its electron-trapping effect. The new characteristics of as-prepared AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 NRs have a larger surface area, charge transport capabilities, and lower recombination chances, associating the synthesized material as a suitable choice for environmental cleanup. The removal of organic dye pollutants from wastewater and the elimination of harmful bacteria by the use of antibacterial activity were the goals of this research. The NRs demonstrated outstanding catalytic activity (CA) against rhodamine B (RhB) dye degradation. AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 nanocatalyst indicates efficient dye degradation (96.39 %) in an acidic medium relative to other basic and neutral media. The fundamental components for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) are electrons and hole pairs responsible for killing pathogenic bacteria. Compared to ciprofloxacin the substantial inhibition zones against Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria were measured at 2.95 mm. In silico molecular docking study of AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 NRs proposed as promising DNA gyrase E. coli inhibitors.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Freshwater resources are constantly being depleted, and the world is facing enormous challenges in meeting the rising demands of fresh water [1]. The expanding human activity and rapid industrial development release harmful organic and inorganic contaminants into the water, putting freshwater supplies and the ecosystem in danger [2]. The organic color dyes, including rhodamine B (RhB), methylene orange, and methylene blue (MB) cause mutagenesis, skin allergies, cancer, and other disorders [3]. RhB serves as a significant colorant in the textile industry due to its excellent stability and lack of biodegradability [4]. Inhaling RhB causes thyroid and liver damage and additionally causes eye and skin irritation [5].

Toxic chemicals and organic pollutants are stable and difficult to eliminate or degrade using conventional methods [6]. Along with anaerobic oxidation, adsorption, photocatalysis activity, and many other techniques have been applied for wastewater treatment [7,8]. Unfortunately, the abovementioned approaches have numerous disadvantages, including limited removal efficiency and selectivity and being expensive, energy-intensive, and ineffective for total dye breakdown or removal [9,10]. Among these, catalytic degradation is efficient because it is energy-efficient, environmentally sustainable, cost-effective, and eliminates or degrades toxic and harmful pollutants [11]. Mastitis has been linked to clinical malformations in mammalian glands and tissues, and abnormalities in the milk's physical and chemical properties [12]. Pathogenic bacteria such as the Gram-positive strain of the pathogen Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and the Gram-negative strain of the pathogen E. coli are responsible for nosocomial infections and diarrhea in humans [13]. E. coli is a well-known and widespread bacterial species. Diarrheagenic E. coli strains are extra-intestinal or enteric pathogens that induce diarrhea in human hosts and clinically display a collection of virulence genes. Diarrhea is a severe problem that kills an estimated 1.3 million children worldwide each year [14,15].

Metal oxide-based semiconductor photocatalysts have demonstrated a promising ability to degrade organic pollutants [16]. Numerous metal oxide-based semiconductors [17], including BiOCl, SnO2, CeO2, NiO2, TiO2, and MnO2, have recently received much attention due to their high stability and low toxicity [18]. MnO2 is regarded as one of the most common and effective catalysts for oxidizing contaminants among other semiconductors [19,20]. MnO2 exists in several polymorphs such as α, β, λ, and δ-type has received a great deal of attention due to its distinctive qualities such as abundance in nature, affordability, non-toxicity, and excellent stability [21,22]. MnO2 is characterized by low conductivity, a weak ion diffusion constant, and poor structural stability [23]. It serves as a catalyst, nanoreactor, and adsorbent, but its catalytic and cycling stability is constrained by low conductivity, insufficient radiation absorption, and a high rate of electron-hole pair recombination [24,25]. The doping has been used to increase surface area, decrease the optical band gap, lessen the risks of electron and hole recombination, and stabilize the structure of synthesized materials [26]. MnO2 nanoparticles have been widely used in catalysis due to their simple manufacturing technique, low cost, strong catalytic activity, and low environmental contamination [27,28].

Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have garnered much attention due to distinctive physicochemical features utilized in catalysis [29]. CDs offer tremendous potential for antibacterial and catalytic activity and can be easily synthesized on a large scale [30]. CDs have low toxicity and quick electron transfer as their defining characteristics. CDs can act as a superconductor and an effective catalyst when coupled with metal oxides [31]. AgBr combined with metal oxides to obtain high degradation activity. The incorporation of AgBr allows the production of electron multiple transfer possible. AgBr has been proposed as a viable catalyst for the degradation of contaminants or the inactivation of germs because of its exceptional optical properties, strong catalytic activity, and good stability [32,33]. In the previous research, a variety of MnO2-doped materials were used to modify the catalytic efficiency. The modification was accomplished by increasing the specific surface area, surface oxygen defects, lattice defects, reactive oxygen concentration, and active sites of MnO2 [34]. RhB exposed to irradiation in the presence of MnO2-modified BaTiO3 ceramics, the aqueous solution of dye degraded 70 % in 360 min [35].

The present work investigates the influence of a fixed concentration of CDs and varying concentrations (2, 4 wt.%) of AgBr as doped materials in MnO2 NRs, synthesized via coprecipitation. The effective novel synthesized AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 was investigated by analyzing the catalytic degradation behavior in the absence of light using NaBH4 as a reducing agent, antibacterial activity, molecular docking analysis, and various characterization techniques to investigate the optical, morphological, and structural analysis of synthesized materials.
Manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O, 99 %), potassium manganate (KMnO4, 99.5 %), potassium bromide (KBr, 98 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.5 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

The corban dots were synthesized using the hydrothermal carbonization method elaborated in Fig. 1 (a). 40 mL of lemon juice was extracted from the lemon, taken into Teflon-lined stainless steel, and autoclaved at 120 °C for 12 h. After the completion of the reaction, the autoclave spontaneously cooled at room temperature, and a solution of a dark brown color was obtained that indicated the formation of CDs. Moreover, the solution has been purified through a filter to remove impurities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and silver bromide doped manganese dioxide
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 M KMnO4 and MnSO4.H2O were used to synthesize MnO2 using coprecipitation method under continuous heating and stirring at 80 °C for 1 h. The required amount of NaOH was added dropwise to obtain the precipitates, and the solution's pH was maintained up to 12. Obtained precipitates were washed with deionized (DI) water using centrifugation to remove impurities. Finally, the prepared sediments were dried for 12 h and ground to get the fine powder. A similar procedure was adopted to synthesize fixed concentrations of CDs with various concentrations (2, 4 wt.%) of silver bromide (AgBr) doped MnO2 indicated in Fig. 1(b).

CA of MnO2 and (2, 4 wt.%) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 was performed against the degradation of RhB dye. Every reagent including RhB and sodium hydroxide (NaBH4) was prepared freshly to ensure an accurate result. The prepared RhB solution at a concentration of 10 mg/L was divided into acidic, basic, and neutral media and the pH of the dye solution was maintained up to 4, 12, and 7, respectively. The prepared 3 mL of dye and 400 µL of NaBH4 were mixed, and 400 µL of synthesized materials were added to examine the level of dye degradation. The surface activation energy of the reaction has been reduced by using a catalyst that increases the reaction's stability and effectiveness. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to perform spectrophotometric measurements at regular intervals to obtain absorption variation spectra. RhB exhibits the absorption wavelength at 554 nm. The degradation of RhB took place, which resulted in a change in color from pink to colorless. The formula used to calculate%age degradation:

where Co is the concentration at the beginning of the dye and Ct is the final concentration of dye after incorporation of the catalyst [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation and identification of MDR E. coli

ce:section.ce:para: Pouring the milk directly into sterile glassware containers. The raw milk samples were collected from nursing cows sold at a variety of marketplaces, and veterinary amenities. The raw milk was delivered to the laboratory as quickly as possible while maintaining a temperature of 4 °C. MacConkey agar was used to count the coliform bacteria present in the raw milk and incubated all of the plates at a temperature of 37 °C for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification and characterization of bacterial isolates
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding Bergey's Manual of Determinative Bacteriology, the preliminary identification of E. coli was determined based on colony morphology, Gram stain, and several biochemical tests [37].

The disk diffusion method using Mueller Hinton agar (MHA) was used to determine antibiotic susceptibility [38]. The experiment was conducted to determine whether or not E. coli was resistant to the aforementioned classes or types of antibiotics: Ceftriaxone (CRO) 30 µg (Cephalosporins), Imipenem (IPM) 10 µg (Carbapenem), Tetracycline (TE) 30 µg (Tetracyclines), Ciprofloxacin (CIP) 5 µg (Quinolones), Septran (SXT) 25 µg, Augmentin (AMC) 30 µg, Gentamicin (CN) 10 µg (Aminoglycosides), Azithromycin (AZM) 15 µg (Macrolides) and Amoxycillin (AXE) 25 μg (Penicillin) [39]. Cultures of E. coli have been purified that were cultivated and raised to a turbidity of 0.5 MacFarland. The infections were spread-plated on MHA (Oxoid Limited, Basingstoke, UK), and the disks containing antibiotics were put sufficiently apart from neighboring ones on the surface of the infected plate to avoid inhibitory zones from overlaying. After incubating the samples on plates at 37 °C for 24 h, the results were evaluated with the help of the Clinical and Laboratory Standard Institute [40]. Microorganisms have been given the label "multidrug-resistant" (MDR) if they have been shown resistant to three or more different types of medication during testing [41].

Using the agar well diffusion method, the in vitro antimicrobial capability of pure and (2, 4 wt.%) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs were investigated on 10 prototypical isolates of MDR E. coli that have been collected from mastitis milk. The isolates were used to test the antibacterial action potential of NRs. Petri dishes have been inoculated with MDR E. coli at a concentration of 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL (0.5 McFarland standard) using MacConkey agar. The wells of 6 mm in width were drilled using a sterile cork borer. High and low concentrations of pure and AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 (1.0 mg/50 µL) and (0.5 mg/50 µL) were utilized to test the antimicrobial activity. The ciprofloxacin concentration of 0.005 mg/50 µL was used as the positive control, while the DI water concentration of 50 µL was used as the negative control [42].

One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed in SPSS 20 [43] using the inhibition zone as the unit of measurement for antimicrobial efficacy. Inhibition zone diameters were analyzed, and the results were considered statistically significant when p<0.05.

Molecular docking analysis was used to explore the binding potential of certain nanostructures (such as MnO2, CDs doped MnO2, and AgBr/CDs doped MnO2) for the DNA gyrase of E. coli. The antibiotic class Ciprofloxacin is very efficient against multidrug-resistant bacteria because it inhibits DNA gyrase. Using the accession number 6L01 (Resolution: 2.1 Å) [44] for DNA gyrase E. coli, the structural characteristics of certain enzyme targets were individually obtained from the protein data bank. As in our previous research [45,46], we utilized the SYBYL-X 2.0 tool to create a 3D structure of compounds and assess the binding affinities of NSs with residues in the active sites of certain proteins.

The crystal structure and phase information of the NRs in 2θ range of 17–70° were determined using a PANalytical XPert PRO X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument with Cu Ka radiation (λ∼ 0.0154 nm). The presence of functional groups in the FTIR spectra was detected using a PerkinElmer spectroscope. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Genesys 10S) was used to investigate the optical characteristics between 330 and 750 nm. The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were captured using a JSM-6460LV FE-SEM system combined with an EDX spectrometer. PL spectra of pure and doped MnO2 were acquired using a JASCO FP-8300 device. The prepared NRs inter-planner d-spacing was measured using the HR-TEM equipment JEOL JEM 2100F.

The hydrothermal carbonization method was used to synthesize CDs indicated in Fig. 1(a). The MnO2 and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 have been synthesized using the coprecipitation method with the fixed concentration of carbon dots and various concentrations (2, 4 wt.%) of AgBr was doped MnO2 represented in Fig. 1(b).

XRD analysis was used to study the structural description of the produced MnO2 and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs in the 2θ range of 17−70° indicated in Fig. 2 (a). The sharp diffraction peaks of MnO2 emerged at an angle 2θ value of 19.1°, 21.8°, 27.5°, 36.8°, 38.8°, 44.4° 55.3° and 66.2° matched (020), (110), (120), (021), (040), (220), (221), and (241) with the crystalline planes respectively. The steep diffraction peaks occur at angles 2θ 30.4° (103), 31.36° (200), 39.8° (105), and 49.5° (312) [47]. Pristine and AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 NRs exhibit the orthorhombic and tetragonal crystal structures, and phase composition composed of MnO2 and Mn3O4 with space group Pbnm and I41/and well matched with the standard JCPDS card # (01–073–1539, 03–065–2776). CDs doping did not result in the formation of a discernible dopant peak or a modification to the crystal structure of MnO2 [48]. CDs indicate the amorphous nature of synthesized material only single diffraction peak observed at an angle 2θ peaks at 24.6° which correspond to (002) in-planes for hexagonal structure [49]. The positions and line widths of the principal XRD peaks can be affected by the composition of a material. Incorporating the CDs, a greater widening of the spacing was seen due to the surface area of the CDs ranging from extremely small to big values [50]. Therefore, the nucleation that occurred during surface growth affected the intensity. The incorporation of AgBr causes the appearance of small peaks at low and high concentrations of AgBr. Strong diffraction peaks of AgBr were found at an angle 2θ value of 26.68 and 55.04 and attributed to (111) and (222) planes [18]. The peak intensity of AgBr planes increased with a high concentration of AgBr. Diffraction peaks shift to higher 2θ values with increasing dopant concentration, with no change in the ionic radius of Mn2+, which may be attributed to a small shift in the crystal lattice constants [51].

Debye-Scherer formula was used to calculate the crystallite (grain) size of synthesized NRs as mentioned below [52]:
(2)

The grain size of MnO2, CDs doped-MnO2, and (2 and 4 %) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 were measured as 14.26, 13.16, 13.75, and 13.47 nm respectively.

The surface area of synthesized NRs was computed using the following equation [53]:
(3)

The surface area of MnO2, CDs doped-MnO2, and (2 and 4 %) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 is 78.35, 91.9, 90.73, and 98.99 m2/g.

Surface properties like specific surface area and surface-to-volume ratio decrease as crystallite size increases [54]. Deformation happens in smaller grains due to changes in the surface state of the boundary, which increases surface area and vice versa [55].

The following relationship was used to compute the percentage crystallinity of as-synthesized NRs [56]:

The crystallinity of pristine MnO2, CDs doped-MnO2, and (2 and 4 %) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 have been calculated as 79.34, 79.08, 66.89, and 69.79 % respectively.

Furthermore, the percentage porosity was calculated using the formula below [56]:
(5)

ρb and ρx are bulk density and density of x-ray respectively.
The percentage porosity of bare MnO2, CDs doped-MnO2, and (2 and 4 %) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 have been measured as 11.76, 19.04, 43.00 and 44.15 % respectively.

FTIR analysis has been used to analyze the presence of different functional groups and chemical structures of pure and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs in the 4000–400 cm−1 range represented in Fig. 2(b). Peaks in the FTIR fall in the range of 400 - 1000 cm−1 could indicate the presence of MnO2. The vibrations of Mn-O bonds appeared at 520 cm−1 associated with MnO2 [57]. The band appeared at 664 cm−1 associated with Mn-O bond as well as hydroxyl groups present in MnO2. The interaction of Mn with OH− and other groups causes the formation of weak bands. The presence of water in the samples stimulates electrochemical activity and produces a distortion of crystal structure in the low-frequency parts of the spectra [58]. The presence of hydrogen-bonded water molecules or hydroxyl groups and hydrogen bound with carboxyl groups associated with water in MnO2 was found at 3370 cm−1 [59]. A weak band observed at 1660 cm−1 was assigned to weak hydrogen bonding of water molecules [60]. MnO2 was synthesized in an aqueous solution and detected the stretching peak of OH. After the insertion of AgBr/CDs, the peaks associated with the vibrations of Mn-O bonds experience a very minor shift toward a higher wavelength. The single-crystal, polycrystalline, or semicrystalline character of synthesized NRs has been determined by implementing the SAED analysis. The synthesized pristine and doped material exhibits bright circular rings (Fig. 2c-f). The bright circular rings provide the perception that the synthesized NRs have a polycrystalline structure. The presence of rings gives a clear indicator that the NRs are exceptionally crystalline in their fundamental structure. The indexed rings provided a satisfactory level of correspondence with the XRD findings.

The FESEM analysis was utilized to identify the surface morphology of pristine and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs indicated in Fig. 3 (a–d). The pristine MnO2 exhibits agglomerated spherical nanoparticle morphology. The strength of Vander Waals forces varies with particle size and ultimately leads to an increase in the surface area of MnO2 nanoparticles as an increasing amount of doped materials [61]. With the incorporation of CDs, the agglomeration and aggregation of CDs doped MnO2 nanoparticles increased. AgBr incorporation indicates the agglomeration increased the chunks-like morphology of spherical nanoparticles grow with the higher concentration of AgBr doped MnO2 spherical nanoparticles. Mapping analysis confirms the presence of elements in the synthesized sample with color indication represented in Fig. 4 (a–d). The presence of manganese (Mn) and oxygen (O) confirms the preparation of MnO2 and indication of sodium (Na) and potassium (K) have been employed to regulate the pH of NRs production, and K is found in KMnO4.

TEM micrographs were used to analyze the topography and morphology of pure and AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 NRs shown in Fig. 5 (a–d). The morphology of the pristine MnO2 was found to be nanorod-shaped. The incorporation of CDs resulted in a dense cluster of nanorod chains. While the incorporation of AgBr the aggregation and agglomeration increase the chain-like morphology of nanorods, the majority of AgBr has been deposited on the surface of MnO2, which has a structure similar to nanorods. AgBr has the potential to boost the growth of MnO2 NRs. TEM validated the formation of AgBr-MnO2 hybrid heterostructures; the AgBr nanorods were coupled with MnO2 NRs. The particle size of synthesized NRs can be calculated with the image j software represented in Fig. 5 (e–h). The particle size is measured as 39.95±10.65, 31.41±6.14, 24.10±6.46 and 20.40 ± 3.89 nm for pristine and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs respectively.

Using Gatan software, the interlayer distance between the lattice fringes of synthesized pure and doped NRs in the HR-TEM micrograph was calculated can be represented in Fig. 6 (a–d). The interlayer d-spacing of pure and doped NRs have been measured as 0.243 nm, 0.242 nm 0.240 nm, and 0.240 nm corresponding to the crystal (021) plane. The AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 exhibit the interlayer d spacing decreased. A decrease in interlayer spacing is observed as heavy metal ions are substituted for those of large ionic radii [62]. The interlayer d-spacing is compatible with JCPDS card number 01–073–1539 and precisely corresponds to the XRD measurement.

EDS analysis has been utilized to investigate the composition of elements in pristine and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs, as represented in Fig. 7 (a–d). The pristine sample indicates the presence of manganese (Mn), oxygen (O), molybdenum (Mo), sodium (Na), potassium (K), and europium (Eu) with the elemental ratios can be indicated in Fig. 7(a). CDs doped MnO2 sample indicates the existence of Corban (C), gold (Au), Mn, O, Mo, Eu, and Na can be indicated in Fig. 7(b). (2, 4 wt.%) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs indicate the presence of the silver (Ag), Bromine (Br), Mn, O, Eu, Au, Pd, and Na can be represented in Fig. 7(c, d). The strong peaks of the elements Mn, O, Ag, and Br indicate that the sample contains AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs. The presence of Au peaks in spectra was discovered to minimize the energy-boosting effect of the Au coating sprinkled on the sample surface. Meanwhile, contamination may be suggested by additional peaks (Eu, Mo, Pd) that may appear due to instrumental error. Na peaks may have formed during the synthesis process due to the use of NaOH to sustain a basic pH.

The optical absorption properties of control and AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 NRs can be investigated using UV–visible spectroscopy with a wavelength range of 330–750 nm can be represented in Fig. 8 (a). The maximum absorption peak of MnO2 has appeared at a wavelength of around 570 nm [63]. The process of charge transfer from O−2 to Mn+4 was triggered by electron excitation from the ground state to the excited state associated with the phenomenon of absorption. The intensity of the absorption band reduced and a shift toward lower absorption was attributed to flaws in the crystal structures of the material. Incorporating 4 wt.% AgBr showed that peaks shifted toward the lower wavelength. The Tauc plot was used to determine the energy band gap of synthesized NRs indicated in Fig. 8(b). The measured values of Eg were 2.17, 2.43, 2.11, and 2.22 eV for pristine and AgBr/CDs -doped materials, respectively indicated in Table 1 . The energy bandgap increased with the incorporation of CDs and decreased with the incorporation of AgBr. AgBr/CDs at concentrations of 2 and 4 % caused the absorption wavelength to gradually shift toward the lower wavelength, demonstrating slightly elevated Eg values due to the quantum confinement effect [64]. AgBr broadens the visible light absorption range, allowing the material to absorb light with longer wavelengths. The photosensitive AgBr synergistically facilitates the separation of photogenerated charge carriers [65]. The equilibration energies of AgBr-doped MnO2 cause band bending of the conduction band, leading to efficient production of the superoxide radical (O2−) [66]. Therefore, incorporating AgBr into a material can effectively decrease the energy band gap and improve its dye degradation performance.

The PL emission spectra were used to investigate the charge transfer, electron-hole recombination rate, and separation efficiency of synthesized NRs in the wavelength range of 360–440 nm (Fig. 8c). The control sample showed the largest emission peak recorded at a wavelength of about 385 nm [67]. The intensity spectrum of the PL emission directly correlates with the recombination rate of the photoinduced charge carriers, which include both electrons and holes. The low intensity in the PL spectrum implies a high charge carrier efficiency and a low rate of photoinduced charge carriers [68,69]. On the other hand, a high intensity in the PL spectrum signifies a high rate of electron-hole pair recombination. Intensity falls with the incorporation of CDs, which indicates that the electron-hole separation rate is increasing. The peak with the highest intensity implies the highest amount of photogenerated charge recombination, while the peaks with lower intensities provide information regarding electron-hole separation [67]. So that the synthesized materials are effective in the breakdown of dye pollution.

The UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to determine the catalytic activity (CA) of MnO2 and (2, 4 wt.%) AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs for the degradation of RhB dye in the presence of NaBH4 in aqueous solution at different pH values (pH∼12, 7 and 4) represented in Fig. 9 (a–c). CA is significantly affected by the pH of the dye solution. The dye pollutants have been released into the environment at various pH levels. The CA of newly manufactured catalysts depends on the pH, which is an important factor in determining the degree to which dyes are degraded. The dye degradation achieved 89.96 %, 63.26 %, 84.58 %, and 90.86 % in a neutral medium at pH∼7. The efficient scalable degradation recorded in the acidic medium can be measured as 59.91 %, 88.17 %, 96.39 %, and 93.78 %. The%age degradation in basic medium (pH∼12) has been observed at 70 %, 64.36 %, 73.27 %, and 61.63 %. The degradation of dye was recorded in all the media after 10 min. Literature comparison of%age degradation with the current study of synthesized NRs can be illustrated in Table 2 . The degradation efficiency is less in the case of MnO2 and increased with the incorporation of AgBr/CDs. An increase in dye degradation was determined in all the media attributed to CDs incorporation associated with an increase in the surface-to-volume ratio for the reaction and a decrease in the particle size. The catalytic activity of the newly synthesized NRs is significantly impacted by the crystallite size, surface area, and shape of the catalyst [70]. The incorporation of AgBr/CDs increased the number of active sites present inside the catalyst, which caused the rate of catalytic degradation to accelerate as a consequence. Therefore, the optimal insertion of AgBr/CDs contributed to increased degradation percentage in an acidic medium.

In addition, AgBr/CDs exhibit a significant and scalable catalytic efficiency for degrading RhB dye in an acidic medium measured as 96.39 %. The most efficient material with the suitable doped material ratio generates electron/hole pairs that can be effectively and efficiently separated due to the interfacial charge transfer [75]. The Ag nanoparticles generated by the breakdown of AgBr act as a bridge for charge migration, ultimately increasing charge separation and enhancing degrading performance [76]. In the basic medium, the degradation was insufficient because the electron-hole pair recombination rate is high and electrons cannot take part in the degradation mechanism of dye, so efficient degradation is not achieved in the basic medium. The 2 % composite has the highest catalytic activity, which is attributed to charge migration. On the other hand, 4 % AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 exhibits less degradation due to the agglomeration and decrease in active sites. The clustering produced by the agglomeration caused a shielding effect that harmed the active sites.

The synthesized nanorods were separated after the degradation of the dye using centrifugation. The heavier NRs settle to the bottom of the centrifuge tube by rapidly spinning the fluid, while the lighter RhB molecules stay in the supernatant. The supernatant can be gently decanted after centrifugation, leaving the NRs behind. The recycling test for catalytic degradation of organic dye was carried out using washed synthesized NRs. The percentage degradation reduced as the number of cycles tested rose [17], which can be represented in Fig. 9(d).

The N-deethylation and dissolution of conjugated structures were frequently utilized for the degradation of RhB dye [77]. The removal of a conjugated xanthene ring in the structure of RhB dye leads to a change in color from pink to nearly colorless [78]. Additionally, benzoid-eliminating intermediates with mass divided by charge (m/z) values of 222, 157, 148, and 147 were identified, which corroborated the decomposition of the conjugated xanthene structure of RhB [79,80]. N-deethylated intermediates were found to have m/z values of 268 and 254, respectively [81]. The intermediates have the potential to undergo further reduction to yield distinct products with m/z values of 148, 118, and 104, respectively [82,83]. Compounds with low molecular weight have the potential to be degraded and mineralized, resulting in the production of carbon dioxide, water, and other inorganic substances. Fig. 10 depicts the realistic mechanism for the degradation of RhB through the interaction of AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 NRs with NaBH4. The generation of the ∗OH radical will be inhibited, which will lead to an increase in the efficiency with which the dye is degraded. In addition, the efficiency of degradation can be increased by the incorporation of AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs and the surface of the nanocatalyst available for adsorption to maximize the degradation efficiency [84].

The antibacterial activity of pure and AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 NRs was evaluated through the agar well diffusion method to analyze the effectiveness against E. coli. The inhibition zones of E. coli for prepared pristine and AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 NRs have been measured in the range of 1.05–2.35 mm for low concentrations and 1.95–2.95 mm for high concentrations against E. coli. In addition, the inhibition zone can be measured at 7.25 mm against E. coli for ciprofloxacin (acts as a positive control) was compared with the inhibition zone of DI water (which acts as a negative control) measured as 0 mm can be indicated in the Table 3 . The pristine and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 NRs exhibit maximum zones of inhibition when the concentration of doped material increased as represented in the Table: 3 the 4 % AgBr/CDs indicate 2.95 mm inhibition zones measurements.

The graphical representation of the inhibition zone measurement of pristine and AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 NRs for high and low inhibition zones compared with ciprofloxacin is indicated in Fig. 11 (a). The%age efficacy for E. coli was determined by dividing the inhibition zone of the sample with the inhibition zones of the positive control. The%age efficacy increases between 14.48 %−32.41 % at low concentrations and 26.89 %−40.68 % at high concentrations, as shown in Fig. 11(b). Antibacterial activity can be affected not only by the concentration of the particles but also by the size of the particles. The small dimensions of particles are responsible for producing a higher number of reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS triggers the expulsion of cytoplasmic components, ultimately resulting in the death of pathogens due to the breaching of the microbial membrane [42].

The antibacterial activity is influenced by the particle size and concentration of the particles. The concentration, size, and shape of a material have a direct impact on the oxidative stress produced by the small-sized particles. There is an inverse relationship between the size of the materials and bactericidal potential. The generation of an increased amount of ROS by nanoparticles, which turn to promotes cytoplasmic components to extrude, which finally contributes to the death of bacteria by implanting damage to the microbe membrane [85]. The mechanism underlying the bactericidal activity of prepared samples containing ROS. ROS are responsible for the death of bacteria by causing oxidative stress on the bacterial cell wall.

Superoxide anion radicals (∗O− 2), hydrogen peroxide radicals (∗H2O2), and hydroxyl radicals (∗OH) are produced in greater quantities by particles of smaller size that cause the death of bacteria. Many transition metal oxides are sportive against microorganisms due to the interaction of hydrophobic and electrostatic forces. The combination of electrons and holes represents the key component in the production of ROS. These species then encourage the ejection of cytoplasmic components, leading to bacterial death due to a rupture in their membrane [86]. The reaction used to address the formation mechanism of ROS can be seen in Fig. 12 .
(6) MnO2 + hν (photon) → h + (hole) + e− (excited electron)
(7)
e − + O2 → ∗O2 −
(8)
h + + H2O → OH− + H+
(9) ∗O− 2 + H + → ∗HO2
(10) ∗HO2 + e− + H+ → ∗H2O2
(11) ∗H2O2 + ∗O− 2 → ∗OH + O2 + OH−

Pristine and AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 NRs are exposed to light with a photon energy equal to the semiconductor's energy bandgap, and electrons from the valence band (VB) are transported to the conduction band (CB), leaving holes in the valence band. The oxygen molecules (O2) on the surface can trap these excited electrons (e) in the CB, and produce superoxide anion radicals. Similarly, the holes in the VB react with water molecules (H2O), releasing hydroxyl and hydrogen ions. Superoxide anion radicals (O― 2) react with H + ions to form hydroperoxyl radicals (∗HO2). The hydroperoxyl radical (HO2) reacts with e and H + to form the hydrogen peroxide radical (∗H2O2). Simultaneously, hydrogen peroxide radicals (∗H2O2) can react with superoxide anion radicals (∗O ― 2) to produce hydroxyl radicals (∗OH), hydroxyl ions (OH−), and singlet oxygen (O2). Negatively charged radicals (hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions) cannot penetrate bacterial cell membranes but can cause significant damage to the bacteria's outer surface, whereas positively charged hydrogen peroxide radicals (∗H2O2) can easily penetrate negatively charged bacterial cell membranes and kill the bacteria. As a result, hydrogen peroxide radical (∗H2O2) is extremely destructive to bacterial cell membranes and causes bacterial death.

NRs form a helical, disorganized spring attached to the cell wall. They pass through the cellular membranes and generate interlinks with the DNA molecular structure. If the cytoplasmic leakage and the permeability of the cell both increase, then it is feasible for the functional groups of ingredients to directly link with the phospholipids present in the cell membrane [87]. Cell death can also occur due to a cationic interaction with a negatively charged cell membrane [88]. The bacterial cell is negatively charged and the synthesized material contains a positive charge on the surface; the interaction between positive and negative charge causes damage to the cell membrane and cell wall of bacteria, which causes cytoplasmic leakage and bacteria death.

It has been discovered that a variety of antibiotics specifically target DNA gyrase, an enzyme that is necessary for the survival of bacteria [89]. In silico analysis was used to examine MnO2, CDs-doped MnO2, and AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 NSs as possible DNA gyrase inhibitors. Fig. 13 shows the NSs binding patterns within the DNA gyrase E. coli active domains. AgBr/CDs doped MnO2 displayed H-bond interactions with Gly77 (2.2 Å) and Thr165 (2.0 Å) of DNA gyrase E. coli , resulting in a binding score of 3.67 presented in Fig. 13(c). Similarly, Fig. 13 (a, b) shows that MnO2 and CDs-doped MnO2 NSs had binding interactions with Thr165 (2.0 Å), with an overall binding score of 2.61 and 2.31, respectively. These docked complexes were also compared to the ciprofloxacin- DNA gyrase E. coli complex represented in Fig. 13(d), a well-studied antibiotic-host cell interaction (binding score: 5.45).

The coprecipitation method was used to synthesize novel pristine MnO2 and AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 to investigate the degradation of organic dye in the absence of light using NaBH4, antimicrobial efficiency against E. coli bacteria, and in silico molecular docking study of DNA gyrase E. coli inhibitors. The XRD patterns endorsed the orthorhombic and tetragonal crystal structure of MnO2 with different phase compositions. 4 % AgBr/CDs-doped MnO2 exhibit the maximum surface area (98.99 m2/g) and porosity (44.14 %). FESEM and TEM micrographs revealed the morphology of agglomerated and aggregated nanorods. The maximum degradation of RhB dye was measured as 96.39, 73.27, and 90.86 % at the optimum concentration of doped materials for acidic, basic, and neutral mediums, respectively. The antimicrobial activity was investigated against E. coli bacteria, and the inhibition zones for low and high doses of NRs (2.35 and 2.95 mm) were measured. DNA gyrase inhibitory activity of AgBr/CDs -doped MnO2 composites was validated by in silico molecular docking studies; these compounds may provide new insights on NRs in E. coli. The findings of the experiments suggest the catalysts utilized in this investigation are effective at removing infectious disease germs and harmful industrial effluents from wastewater.
